items.q: How do you say 'My sister is embarrassed' in Spanish?
items.correct: Mi hermana tiene vergüenza / está avergonzada.
items.why: Notorious mistake when discussing family/relationships. 'Embarazada' = PREGNANT, not embarrassed. To say embarrassed: tener vergüenza or estar avergonzado/a. Saying your sister is pregnant when you mean embarrassed causes laughter.

items.q: In Spain, how do you address a group of friends informally?
items.why: Students don't distinguish vosotros (informal plural, Spain) from ustedes (formal plural in Spain, but used for all plural in Latin America). In Spain: tú/vosotros = informal; usted/ustedes = formal. Latin America: only ustedes for plural.

items.correct: Could be his/her/your(formal)/their brother - context determines which.
items.why: Students assume su = his. SU is one of Spanish's most context-dependent words. Means his, her, your (usted/ustedes), or their. Speakers often clarify with 'de él', 'de ella', 'de usted' if ambiguous: 'el hermano de él'.
Correct answer: la hermana. “el hermano” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: mi abuelo. “mi abuela” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many grandparents (abuelos) does a person typically have?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
